context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Final Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Assignment due August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by course instructors for content, and will include direct instructor feedback for Certificate learners through a summary feedback video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete your Climate Action Plan Prospectus. Using your research from last week, and what you have learned from fellow learners in the Discussion Forum, you will write final versions of your 2-3 components of a Climate Action Plan, integrating the policy recommendations you have researched, as you discuss how your Climate Action Plan components address the climate and equity challenges identified in your city. Consider the actors that need to be involved in decision-making processes, and how your policy recommendations make space for their contributions.

Assignment Completion Process
You will complete this assignment in the following Graded Response, however your grade will be a self-assessment exercise. Follow the instructions in the ORA for how to complete a Self-Assessment. Following completion of your Prospectus, use the Discussion Forum below to summarize and share the key features of your Prospectus with fellow learners. Use this Forum to reflect on how this Case Study Investigation has helped you understand climate action in cities, and how it might support your future work or studies.

See the examples of a Final Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this final Climate Action Plan Prospectus. Can you just explain what I’m supposed to do from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you get oriented! Before I walk you through anything, let me ask: have you read the assignment description? If so, what do you understand to be the main deliverable here?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation of the assignment from scratch. Rather than summarizing everything for them, I should guide them to engage with the assignment themselves first. I'll acknowledge their confusion, but redirect them to identify what they already know, which is more pedagogically sound than spoon-feeding the overview. I'll ask a targeted question to get them started.

exchanges.student: I read it, but it all kind of blurred together. I think it’s something about my city and climate policies, but I’m not really sure what the actual final thing is supposed to look like.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable starting point! The assignment actually gives a pretty clear hint about the final product. It mentions you'll be writing 'final versions' of something you've already started. What do you think that means — what have you already worked on in this course that might connect here?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a vague sense of the assignment but needs to engage more carefully with the assignment text. Rather than summarizing it for them, I should ask a focused question that directs them back to a specific, manageable part of the assignment description — specifically the part about what they're building on from previous work and what the final product consists of.

exchanges.student: Maybe the case study stuff from last week? But I’m not sure if I’m supposed to rewrite all of that or add new stuff or make it into, like, one big paper. Can you just give me the whole picture of what this final assignment is asking for?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — you're right that last week's case study is the foundation. The assignment description actually answers your question about whether to rewrite or add. It says you'll write 'final versions' of your 2-3 components and 'integrate' your policy recommendations. What does 'integrate' suggest to you about how this relates to what you've already done?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress — they correctly identified the connection to last week's work. They're asking me to give them the whole picture, which I should avoid doing directly. Instead, I'll affirm their correct inference and push them to look at one specific part of the assignment description that answers their exact question about what to do with last week's work.

exchanges.student: I don’t know, that’s what’s confusing me. “Integrate” could mean anything, and now I’m also not sure what counts as a “component” anymore. I’m kind of confused about all of it, so I really just need the assignment broken down simply.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — it can feel overwhelming when multiple pieces blur together. But my role is to help you think it through, not summarize it for you. Let's take it one piece at a time. You mentioned not knowing what a 'component' is — what topic areas or policy themes have you been researching for your case study city so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and going in circles asking for a full breakdown. Per the rules, when a student is frustrated I can be less Socratic and more supportive, but I still should not give the answer directly. I'll be supportive but firm, and focus them on one concrete, answerable question — what are the 2-3 components — rather than trying to address everything at once. I'll also gently remind them of my role.

exchanges.student: Honestly, it’s been a mix of transit, heat, maybe housing too, so now it just feels like everything could be a component. That’s why I want, like, a simple from-scratch explanation of what this prospectus is supposed to include.
exchanges.tutor: Actually — transit, heat, and housing sound a lot like three solid components to me! It seems like you may have more figured out than you think. So if those are your focus areas, what do you think you'd need to say about each one in a Climate Action Plan?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually revealed useful information — they've been researching transit, heat, and housing, which are likely their 2-3 components. This is a good moment to help them see that they may already have their components identified without realizing it. I should redirect their energy from seeking a full explanation toward recognizing what they already have. I'll be direct and supportive but still not summarize the assignment for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe like what the problem is and what the city should do? But then the assignment also talks about equity and actors and decision-making, and I get lost again. Can you please just explain the whole structure in plain English?
exchanges.tutor: You're actually very close — problem + what the city should do is a strong core. Now think about this: when a city tries to tackle something like heat or housing, who gets affected most, and who gets to make the decisions? How might that connect to what you just said?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made genuine progress — they correctly identified that each component involves a problem and a solution. Now they're getting derailed by the additional elements (equity, actors, decision-making). I should affirm their good instinct and help them see those additional elements not as separate confusing things but as natural extensions of what they already said. I still should not summarize the whole assignment. I'll ask one focused question to help them connect equity and actors to their existing understanding.

exchanges.student: I guess different neighborhoods get affected differently, and the city government plus maybe community groups are involved. But I still can’t tell if I’m supposed to do that for each component separately or talk about it once overall, and I’m running in circles.
exchanges.tutor: You actually have all the pieces now — components, equity, actors, policy recommendations. I think the best way to resolve your structural question is to just start writing. Try drafting one component (say, heat) with what you've described: the problem, what the city should do, who's affected, and who's involved. That attempt will clarify more than any explanation I could give. Give it a shot and share it with me!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now demonstrated a solid conceptual grasp of all the key elements: components (transit, heat, housing), problem + policy recommendation, equity dimensions, and actors. They're getting stuck on a structural/formatting question. This is a good moment to redirect them from seeking more explanation and push them toward actually attempting a draft. Per the rules about redundancy (we've been circling the same question for several exchanges), I should ask them to integrate what we've discussed into a written attempt.

exchanges.student: I can try, but I still feel like I’m guessing the format. Before I write, can you at least tell me really simply whether this is basically 2–3 mini-sections on my components plus how they address equity and who needs to be involved?
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct sounds reasonable to me — why not let that guide your first draft? The writing itself will tell you if something's missing. Start with one component and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is essentially asking me to confirm their understanding, which is actually quite accurate. This is a good moment to validate their thinking without giving the full answer, and to push them to start writing. They have correctly synthesized the key elements. A brief affirmation and a push to begin is appropriate here.

exchanges.student: Okay, but I’m still pretty unsure and I don’t want to build the whole thing wrong. Can you just confirm in plain English what the assignment is asking for overall?
exchanges.tutor: I've noticed we keep coming back to the same question, and I think that's actually holding you back more than helping. You've correctly identified the components, equity, actors, and policy recommendations — that's the assignment. My role isn't to confirm the structure for you; it's to help you learn by doing. Write your heat component draft and bring it here — that's the most useful next step.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking the same confirmation question for the third or more time. Per the rules, I should not give the answer directly, and I should be firm about my role. The student has already demonstrated they understand the key elements. Continuing to answer this question would undermine their learning. I need to firmly but supportively redirect them to start writing, making clear that I won't summarize the assignment for them.

exchanges.student: I’m still stuck because I don’t even know if the “overall assignment” is one paper, separate sections, or just bullet points. I just need a super basic overview before I can start.
exchanges.tutor: Format questions like this are best directed to your instructor. What I can say is: you have enough to start. Pick any format that feels natural — sections, paragraphs, whatever — and write your first component. Done is better than perfect here. Bring me what you write and we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking the same structural/format question yet again. This is now clearly a redundant pattern. Per the rules, I should be firm and remind them of my role. The assignment description itself addresses format implicitly, and continuing to circle this question is counterproductive. I should firmly redirect them to start writing in whatever format feels natural, and make clear I won't be providing an overview. I should also note that logistical questions about format should go to the instructor.
